section.title: The Day of the LORD and Israel's Restoration
section.sectionRationale: The book turns from Edom to the whole world and from judgment to hope. The Day of the LORD is near for all the nations, the principle of recompense, as you have done it will be done to you, is stated, and then the vision opens onto Mount Zion delivered and holy, the house of Jacob restored and expanding, and a final word that gathers everything: the kingdom will belong to the LORD.
section.thesis: The Day of the LORD comes for all the nations on the principle of exact recompense; Edom is consumed, but Zion is delivered and holy, God's people are restored, and the whole vision resolves in the reign of the LORD Himself.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Day of the LORD and the law of recompense; the deliverance and enlargement of Jacob against Esau's stubble; the restored borders climaxing in deliverers on Zion and the kingdom of the LORD.

units.label: The Day of the LORD and recompense
units.note: as you have done, it will be done to you

verses.text: For the {{yomYHWH|Day of the LORD}} is near for all the nations. As you have done, it will be done to you; your recompense will return upon your own head.
For as you drank on My holy mountain, so all the nations will drink continually. They will drink and gulp it down; they will be as if they had never existed.

units.label: Deliverance for Zion, fire against Esau
units.note: holy, and the house of Jacob restored

verses.text: But on Mount Zion there will be deliverance, and it will be {{qodesh|holy}}, and the house of Jacob will reclaim their possession.

Then the house of Jacob will be a blazing fire, and the house of Joseph a burning flame; but the house of Esau will be stubble; Jacob will set it ablaze and consume it. Therefore no survivor will remain from the house of Esau.” For the LORD has spoken.

units.label: Restored borders and the kingdom of the LORD

verses.text: Those from the Negev will possess the mountains of Esau; those from the foothills will possess the land of the Philistines. They will occupy the fields of Ephraim and Samaria, and Benjamin will possess Gilead.
And the exiles of this host of the Israelites will possess the land of the Canaanites as far as Zarephath; and the exiles from Jerusalem who are in Sepharad will possess the cities of the Negev.
The {{moshiim|deliverers}} will ascend Mount Zion to rule over the mountains of Esau. And the {{meluchah|kingdom}} will belong to the LORD.

yomYHWH.gloss: The Day of the LORD, a recurring prophetic phrase for the appointed time when God acts decisively to judge the wicked and deliver His people. It is both a near historical reckoning and a picture of the final judgment. In Obadiah it widens the scope from Edom to all the nations and grounds the promise that wrong will be exactly repaid.

qodesh.gloss: Holiness, that which is set apart and sacred to God. Mount Zion, defiled when foreigners entered its gates, will be holy again, cleansed and consecrated as the LORD's own place. The word points beyond geography to a people and a city belonging wholly to God.

moshiim.gloss: Deliverers, saviours, those who rescue and set free, from the verb yasha, to save. The same root gives the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD saves. In Obadiah the deliverers ascend Zion to rule, a role that finds its fullness in the one great Saviour, Jesus Christ.

meluchah.gloss: Kingship, kingdom, royal rule. The book's final word declares that the kingdom, the sovereign rule over all, belongs to the LORD. Every rival power, Edom and the nations included, gives way to the one true King, a hope fulfilled in the reign of Christ.

basic.context: The book now lifts its eyes from Edom to all the nations and from judgment to rescue. The Day of the LORD is coming, when everyone is repaid for what they have done. Edom and the nations are consumed, but Mount Zion is delivered and made holy, God's people are restored to their land and beyond, and the book ends with the greatest note of all: the kingdom belongs to the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic vision of God's future, mixing the near restoration of Israel with a picture of the final Day of the LORD. The rebuilt borders in verses 19 and 20 were partly fulfilled in history, but the sweep of the passage, all nations judged and God reigning, points beyond any single event to Christ's kingdom.
basic.originalLanguages: The Day of the LORD, yom YHWH, is the appointed time when God judges and saves. Zion will be holy, qodesh, set apart to God again. The deliverers, moshiim, come from the word to save, the same root as the name Jesus. And the last word, kingdom, meluchah, declares that the rule belongs to the LORD.
basic.typology: The delivered and holy Mount Zion points to the church and the heavenly Jerusalem. The deliverers who save and reign foreshadow Christ the great Deliverer, and the kingdom of the LORD is the reign He brings and shares with His people.
basic.christ: This passage points straight to Jesus. He is the true Deliverer who ascends Zion to reign; His name means the LORD saves. The Day of the LORD is fulfilled in His coming to judge and to save, and the final line, the kingdom will belong to the LORD, is realised in Christ's reign, now and at His return.
basic.god: We learn that God is perfectly just, repaying exactly what has been done, and perfectly faithful, delivering and restoring His people. Above all we learn that He is King; history is heading toward the moment when His rule is unrivalled and complete.
basic.crossReferences: Joel and Amos speak of the Day of the LORD and Zion restored. Revelation 11:15 echoes the last line, the kingdom of the world becomes the LORD's. Galatians 3:29 makes believers heirs of the promises to Jacob.
basic.oneStory: Obadiah ends where the whole Bible is heading: every wrong repaid, God's people delivered and holy, and the kingdom belonging to the LORD. That final line reaches its fulfilment in Christ, in whom God's reign comes and will one day fill everything.

context.literary: The book pivots at verse 15 from Edom in particular to all the nations, and from judgment to salvation. The law of recompense, as you have done it will be done to you, governs the turn: Edom's treachery in verses 10 to 14 now rebounds on its own head. Images of drinking the cup, of Jacob as fire and Esau as stubble, and of ever-expanding borders build toward the soaring last line, and the kingdom will belong to the LORD, the theological summit of the book.
context.historical: The restoration envisioned Israel repossessing its land and spreading in every direction, into the mountains of Esau, Philistia, Ephraim, Gilead, and beyond to Zarephath and Sepharad, places marking the far reach of the exile. Edom itself was overrun by the Nabateans and others, its people later absorbed, so that as a nation it disappeared, matching no survivor will remain. Historically, John Hyrcanus subdued the remaining Edomites, the Idumeans, in the second century BC.
context.canonical: The Day of the LORD is a great prophetic theme, sounded in Joel, Amos, Zephaniah, and elsewhere, both near and final. The closing declaration, the kingdom will belong to the LORD, joins the Bible's central hope of God's reign, taken up in the Psalms, in Daniel's everlasting kingdom, and climactically in Revelation 11:15, the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To widen the horizon from Edom to all nations under the coming Day of the LORD, to assure God's afflicted people of deliverance, restoration, and holiness on Mount Zion, and to lift every eye to the final truth that the kingdom belongs to the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes and promises what God will do; it is prophecy and assurance, not a command to be obeyed. The abiding truths are the certainty of God's righteous recompense, the sure deliverance of His people, and the ultimate, unrivalled reign of the LORD.

debates.question: How is the restoration and the kingdom of the LORD fulfilled?

views.label: Fulfilled in Christ and His kingdom
views.case: The New Testament sees the promises to Jacob realised in Christ and His people, the true Zion and heavenly Jerusalem, Hebrews 12:22, with the reign of the LORD inaugurated at Christ's first coming and consummated at His return, Revelation 11:15.

views.label: A future national restoration of Israel
views.case: Some read the repossessed borders and the rule over Esau's mountains as awaiting a literal, future restoration of national Israel in the land, with these promises fulfilled in a coming age.

debates.bearing: Both hold that God keeps His word to deliver and restore, and both confess that the last line settles everything: whatever the shape of fulfilment, the kingdom belongs to the LORD, secured in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The climax gathers the book's key words. Yom YHWH, the Day of the LORD, names the appointed reckoning, now widened from Edom to all the nations, and governed by exact recompense, as you have done it will be done to you. The picture of drinking on My holy mountain becomes the cup all nations must drink. Then hope breaks in: Zion will be qodesh, holy, set apart to God again after its defiling. Jacob becomes fire and Esau stubble, reversing the earlier ruin. Finally the moshiim, the deliverers or saviours, ascend Zion; the word shares the root yasha, to save, from which the name Jesus comes. And the last word is meluchah, kingship: the kingdom will belong to the LORD.

typology.fulfillment: The church and the heavenly Jerusalem, the redeemed people set apart to God, Hebrews 12:22-24; 1 Peter 2:9.

typology.type: The deliverers who ascend Zion to rule
typology.fulfillment: Christ the great Deliverer, whose name means the LORD saves, reigning and sharing His rule with His people, Revelation 2:26-27.

typology.type: The kingdom belonging to the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The reign of God established in Christ, inaugurated at His coming and consummated at His return, Revelation 11:15.

study.christ: This closing vision runs straight to Jesus. The deliverers who ascend Mount Zion are moshiim, saviours, from the root yasha, to save, the very root of the name Jesus, the LORD saves; He is the Deliverer they foreshadow, who goes up to reign. The Day of the LORD, near for all the nations, is fulfilled in His coming to judge the world in righteousness and to rescue His own, Acts 17:31. Mount Zion made holy is the redeemed people He purifies as His own, and the heavenly Jerusalem to which believers have come, Hebrews 12:22. And the final line is the goal of all history, echoed in Revelation 11:15: the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, and He will reign forever.
study.god: The LORD is perfectly just, repaying each exactly as they have done, so that the cup Edom made others drink it must drink itself. He is faithful, delivering Zion, restoring His people, and enlarging their inheritance beyond anything they lost. He is holy, cleansing His mountain and setting it apart to Himself. And supremely He is King: the whole book, and all of history, resolves in the truth that the kingdom belongs to the LORD, His reign unrivalled and everlasting.

crossReferences.note: On Mount Zion there will be deliverance, quoted almost word for word, tied to calling on the name of the LORD, Acts 2:21.
The restored booth of David possessing the remnant of Edom and all the nations, cited at the Jerusalem council, Acts 15:16-17.
The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, the fulfilment of Obadiah's final line.
Believers have come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem, the reality toward which the delivered and holy Zion points.

study.oneStory: Obadiah ends where the whole Bible is heading. The Day of the LORD repays every wrong, God's people are delivered, restored, and made holy on Zion, and the last line lifts the eye to the summit: the kingdom will belong to the LORD. That reign comes in Jesus the Deliverer, whose name means the LORD saves, and it will fill all things when the kingdom of the world becomes the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ.

sermon.why: No sermon on Obadiah 1:15-21 from a trusted expositor could be confirmed by web search in this session, so none is listed.

quiz.q: What principle governs the Day of the LORD in verse 15?

options.text: Exact recompense: as you have done, it will be done to you.
options.note: Verse 15 states that one's own deeds return upon one's own head.

options.text: Unconditional mercy for every nation regardless of conduct.
options.note: Mercy is offered to God's people, but the Day here is one of just recompense.

options.text: Random calamity with no moral order.
options.note: The judgment is precisely measured to what was done, not random.

options.text: Reward based on national size and strength.
options.note: It is based on deeds, not on might; the mighty Edom is brought down.

quiz.q: What is the great note on which the book of Obadiah ends?

options.text: The kingdom will belong to the LORD.
options.note: Verse 21 closes with the LORD's unrivalled reign, the summit of the book.

options.text: Edom will finally be forgiven and restored.
options.note: Edom is consumed as stubble; the closing hope is the LORD's kingdom.

options.text: Israel will build a great army.
options.note: Israel is restored, but the climax is God's reign, not military might.

options.text: The nations will remain in power forever.
options.note: The opposite: every rival power gives way to the LORD's kingdom.

quiz.q: Who are the ‘deliverers’ who ascend Mount Zion, and to whom do they point?

options.text: Saviours, from the root yasha, to save; they foreshadow Christ, whose name means the LORD saves.
options.note: Moshiim shares the root that gives the name Jesus; He is the great Deliverer who reigns.

options.text: Foreign kings who conquer Zion.
options.note: They ascend to rule for God's people, not foreign conquerors.

options.text: Priests who offer sacrifices only.
options.note: Their role is to deliver and rule, pointing beyond priesthood to Christ the Saviour-King.

options.text: Angelic beings with no link to Christ.
options.note: The word means human saviours and points to Christ, not angels.

quiz.q: What happens to Mount Zion in the restoration?

options.text: There will be deliverance, it will be holy, and Jacob will reclaim its possession.
options.note: Verse 17 promises deliverance, holiness, and restored inheritance on Zion.

options.text: It will be abandoned and left in ruins.
options.note: The opposite: Zion is delivered and made holy again.

options.text: It will be handed over permanently to Edom.
options.note: Edom becomes stubble; Zion is delivered, not handed to Edom.

options.text: It will remain defiled as it was on the day it fell.
options.note: It was defiled, but the promise is that it will be holy once more.

quiz.q: How is Obadiah's closing hope fulfilled in the New Testament?

options.text: In Christ's reign, echoed in Revelation 11:15, the kingdom of the world becomes the LORD's and His Christ's.
options.note: The final line finds its fulfilment in the kingdom of God established in Christ.

options.text: In the rebuilding of Edom as a Christian nation.
options.note: Edom is consumed; the fulfilment is Christ's kingdom, not Edom's revival.

options.text: Only in a political state, with no link to Christ.
options.note: There may be a historical dimension, but the New Testament centres fulfilment on Christ's reign.

options.text: It is left entirely unfulfilled in the New Testament.
options.note: The New Testament takes up this hope directly in Christ's kingdom.

questions: The Day of the LORD repays each according to their deeds. How does the certainty that God will one day set every wrong right change the way you face injustice now?
Zion was defiled but promised to be holy again. Where do you long for God to cleanse and set apart something in your life that has been spoiled?
The deliverers point to Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves. What would it mean to trust Him more fully as your Deliverer this week?
The book ends with the kingdom belonging to the LORD. In what area are you still living as if the kingdom belonged to you, and how might you yield it to His reign?

God turned Edom's treachery back on its own head yet lifted His people to a restored and holy home. How does this move you to trust God with those who have wronged you rather than repaying them yourself?

translationNotes.note: As you drank on My holy mountain uses the image of the cup: Judah drank the cup of suffering when Jerusalem fell, and now all the nations must drink the cup of God's judgment continually until they are as if they had never existed.
The BSB prints an em dash before Jacob will set it ablaze; this has been rendered with a semicolon per house style. For the LORD has spoken seals the certainty of the whole oracle.

The foothills renders a term the BSB footnote gives as the Shephelah or lowlands, the western foothills of Judea; the verse pictures God's people expanding in every direction into surrounding territories.

The deliverers will ascend renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as the deliverers will go up from, and the Septuagint as those being delivered will go up; the sense of saviours ascending Zion to rule stands in every reading, climaxing in the kingdom belonging to the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Obadiah 1:15-21. The BSB footnotes at verses 19 and 21 (Shephelah; the deliverers will go up from / LXX those being delivered) are reflected in the translation notes. The em dash printed by the BSB in verse 18 has been rendered as a semicolon per house style, with no change to wording.
